comparisons.topic: The Ancestral Homeland of the Bantu

biblical.evidence: Genesis 10 geography places Cush in the East. Proto-Bantu vocabulary points to highland savannas (cattle, sorghum). Oral traditions consistently point to origins in the North or East.

conventional.evidence: Places the homeland in the Congo-Niger basin near Cameroon, based on the assumption that the region with the highest linguistic diversity is the point of origin.

comparisons.resolution: High linguistic diversity can be explained by founder effects or the Babel paradigm rather than deep time. The combination of oral memory, agricultural evidence, and Scripture strongly supports an Eastern/Northern origin.

biblical.evidence: Genesis 4 reveals metallurgy existed before the Flood. Iron working in Africa was a recovery and transmission of ancient knowledge, explaining its rapid spread among early pioneers.

conventional.evidence: Humanity slowly evolved from stone tools to bronze and eventually to iron smelting through independent discovery and long periods of trial and error.

comparisons.resolution: The sophisticated nature of early African furnaces (like the Urewe forced-draft furnaces) points to the application of pre-existing knowledge rather than a slow evolutionary ascent from primitive states.

biblical.evidence: Adjusts C-14 dates by accounting for lower post-Flood atmospheric carbon levels. A rapid exponential population growth (like the Israelites in Egypt) easily fills the continent in ~1,000 years.

conventional.evidence: Relies on uncalibrated C-14 dates from organic remains like charcoal, assuming atmospheric carbon ratios have remained constant over tens of thousands of years.

comparisons.resolution: When we recalibrate carbon dates to account for the catastrophic changes of the Flood, archaeological cultures like Urewe fit perfectly within the biblical chronology of the post-Babel world.
